headline: MEAL ROCKETS 2.3% ON CHINESE CRUSH DEMAND
subheadline: Soybeans follow meal higher while precious metals crater on dollar strength.
lead: Soybean meal exploded 2.3% overnight as Chinese crushers scrambled for protein ahead of their seasonal rebuild. The meal rally dragged soybeans up 8½¢ despite lackluster export inspections. Meanwhile, gold and silver got hammered — down 4.1% and 4.4% respectively — as the dollar flexed and speculators fled commodities.
teaser: Meal rockets on Chinese demand while metals crash — here's what it means for your marketing.

one_number.context: That's a $7.50 overnight jump — the biggest single-day gain since January. Chinese processors are paying up for immediate protein as their livestock sector rebuilds post-disease outbreak. This pulls value away from oil and into meal, boosting your bean basis.

sections.title: GRAINS & OILSEEDS
sections.body: Soybeans rode meal's coattails up 8½¢, but the real story is the protein premium explosion. Meal gained $7.50 while oil barely budged, widening the crush spread to levels that make processors drool. Corn tagged along for a 5¾¢ gain on feed substitution fears — when meal gets expensive, feeders eye corn gluten. Wheat added 5¢ in sympathy, though Black Sea competition remains fierce. The meal surge signals Chinese demand is real, not just trade war posturing.
sections.bottom_line: Meal shortage pulls beans higher — oil stays weak.
sections.farmer_action: Consider pricing 10-15% of new-crop beans on this meal-driven rally — protein premiums won't last forever.

sections.title: LIVESTOCK & DAIRY
sections.body: Cattle and hogs got crushed while feed costs surged — a nightmare scenario for margins. Feeder cattle dropped 1.9% as meal's jump threatens backgrounding profits. Live cattle fell 1.1% as packers backed off amid weak retail demand. Hogs suffered worst, down 2.5% on heavy supplies and export uncertainty. But milk rallied 2.1% as spring flush delays and strong cheese demand supported prices. The feed cost squeeze is real — meal up $7.50 while cattle sink.
sections.bottom_line: Feed costs surge while meat prices sink — margins getting squeezed hard.
sections.farmer_action: Lock in milk pricing if you can — feed costs are climbing faster than meat prices.

sections.title: ENERGY & INPUTS
sections.body: Crude oil tumbled 2.5% to $94 as recession fears trumped geopolitical risk. Natural gas followed suit, down 2.5% as mild weather forecasts reduced heating demand. The energy selloff should ease some input cost pressure — diesel and fertilizer pricing typically follow crude with a 2-week lag. But transportation costs remain elevated compared to pre-2024 levels. Natgas weakness is especially welcome for nitrogen producers, though ammonia plants won't pass savings through immediately.
sections.bottom_line: Energy weakness offers first input cost relief in months.
sections.farmer_action: Watch for diesel price drops in 1-2 weeks — good timing for spring fieldwork prep.

sections.title: MACRO & TRADE
sections.body: The dollar held flat at 100.00 while precious metals imploded — gold down 4.1%, silver off 4.4% in a spectacular flight from inflation hedges. Chinese meal buying shows their economy isn't as weak as headlines suggest, but U.S. export competitiveness remains challenged by dollar strength. Stock market weakness (S&P down 0.3%) reflects growing recession concerns that could crater commodity demand. The metals crash suggests inflation fears are fading — bad for ag as a hedge play.
sections.bottom_line: Strong dollar hurts exports while metals crash signals fading inflation fears.
sections.farmer_action: Export timing matters more than ever — dollar strength makes U.S. grain less competitive globally.

the_more_you_know.title: Why Meal Rallies Matter More Than Bean Rallies
the_more_you_know.body: When soybean meal surges like today's 2.3% move, it changes the entire soybean complex math. Processors make money by crushing beans into meal and oil — when meal prices spike, they'll pay more for beans to capture that margin. But meal rallies also signal tight protein supplies globally, which can persist for months. Oil rallies, by contrast, often fade quickly since there's usually plenty of vegetable oil around.

watch_list.desc: Chinese soybean purchase announcements — meal rally needs follow-through buying
Export inspections — need to see if this demand surge shows up in shipments
Diesel prices at local pumps — crude's 2.5% drop should filter through
USDA Prospective Plantings — the big enchilada for acreage intentions
Field conditions for early nitrogen applications — timing is everything

daily_quote.text: Rain makes grain — except when it doesn't stop.

source_summary: CME Group · Chinese Futures Markets · USDA · Federal Reserve · Energy Information Administration
